text.title: api-monitoring-alerting Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding API Monitoring — Alerting. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: SLO MUST be defined before any alert rule is written. The SLO document MUST specify: target percentage, measurement window (e.g., 99.9% over 30 days), and error budget. Alert thresholds MUST be derived from the error budget using burn-rate windows, not chosen arbitrarily.
rule.rationale: Alert thresholds plucked from air (5xx > 1%) are arbitrary and generate constant false positives. Tying every threshold to a stated SLO means alerts fire only when the business commitment is actually at risk. SLO numbers are business decisions — require PM or ops sign-off before setting targets.

rule.statement: Every Prometheus alert rule MUST have: for: clause of at least 2 minutes (never 0s or absent), a severity label with value page or ticket, and a runbook_url annotation. Alert rules without runbook_url MUST be rejected by CI (promtool check rules + grep check).
rule.rationale: A for: 0s alert fires on a single bad scrape (one anomalous 15s window). At least 2 minutes means the condition must persist across two scrape cycles. Missing runbook_url turns every alert into a context-free interrupt. Block in CI with the lint script.

rule.statement: Multi-window multi-burn-rate pattern MUST be used for all SLO-protecting alert rules. The page-level rule fires when 2% of the 30-day budget burns in 1h AND 5% in 6h. The ticket-level rule fires when 10% burns in 3d AND 10% in 1d. NEVER use single-window threshold alerts as the primary SLO protection.
rule.rationale: Single-window alerts have two failure modes: too sensitive (fire on transient spikes that don't threaten the SLO) and too slow (miss slow burns that erode the budget over days). Multi-window burn-rate catches both: it is sensitive to fast burns and accumulates signal for slow ones. Reference: Google SRE Workbook chapter 5.

rule.statement: Alert routing MUST match severity: `page` severity routes to PagerDuty/on-call; `ticket` severity routes to Jira/Linear; no severity → reject. Never page on ticket severity, never ticket on page severity.

rationale.source: PagerDuty severity routing patterns; Google SRE workbook

rule.rationale: Mis-routed alerts either wake people for non-incidents or fail to wake them for real ones. Severity labels MUST be the single source of truth for routing.

rule.statement: Grafana dashboards MUST be managed as code via jsonnet or `grafanactl pull`; hand-edited dashboard JSON is forbidden in main.
rule.rationale: Hand-edited JSON produces formatting churn and merge conflicts; dashboards-as-code give PR review, history, and rollback via git revert.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the team has no SLO target or when alerts are exploratory (POC environment with no production traffic).
rule.rationale: Burn-rate alerts require an SLO anchor; without it, every threshold is arbitrary. POC environments don't benefit from page-grade alerting.
